description: A structured review using pre-defined eligibility criteria to identify, appraise, and synthesize all relevant studies on a specific question, following PRISMA reporting guidelines.
when_to_use: When you need a comprehensive, reproducible summary of all existing evidence on a focused research question; standard for medical and policy decision-making.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria and critical appraisal seek to minimize bias and identify weak studies.

description: A systematic mapping of the breadth and key concepts of a research area, typically following the Arksey & O'Malley (2005) framework or JBI methodology. Addresses exploratory questions rather than testing hypotheses.
when_to_use: When the research area is complex, heterogeneous, or emerging, and you need to clarify concepts, identify knowledge gaps, or determine whether a full systematic review is warranted.
seeks_refutation: Partially — seeks to identify gaps and map coverage rather than formally test or refute a hypothesis.
source_name: Arksey & O'Malley (2005) framework; PRISMA-ScR extension for scoping reviews

description: A form of knowledge synthesis in which components of the systematic review process are simplified or omitted to produce information in a timely manner (Khangura et al., 2012).
when_to_use: When time-sensitive evidence is needed for policy or clinical decisions and a full systematic review is not feasible due to resource or time constraints.
seeks_refutation: Yes — still applies critical appraisal but with acknowledged limitations in search breadth and depth.
source_name: Khangura et al. (2012) Evidence summaries: the evolution of a rapid review approach — Systematic Reviews journal

description: An empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary phenomenon within its real-life context, especially when boundaries between phenomenon and context are not clearly evident (Yin, 2018).
when_to_use: When you want an in-depth understanding of a complex phenomenon in its real-world setting; useful for answering 'how' and 'why' questions.
seeks_refutation: No — primarily seeks to build or refine theory through detailed description and explanation, not falsification.
source_name: Robert K. Yin (2018) Case Study Research and Applications: Design and Methods (6th ed.)

description: An inductive theory-building method where theory is generated systematically from data collected and analyzed through constant comparison and theoretical sampling (Glaser & Strauss, 1967).
when_to_use: When no existing theory adequately explains a phenomenon and you want to build a new theory grounded in empirical data collected from the field.
seeks_refutation: No — seeks to construct theory from data rather than test or refute a pre-existing hypothesis; emphasizes emergent theory.
source_name: Barney G. Glaser & Anselm L. Strauss (1967) The Discovery of Grounded Theory: Strategies for Qualitative Research

description: A qualitative method rooted in anthropology involving prolonged immersion in a community or setting to understand cultural patterns, meanings, and practices from an emic (insider) perspective via participant observation.
when_to_use: When you need a deep, contextual understanding of a culture, community, or social practice that cannot be captured by surveys or experiments.
seeks_refutation: No — primarily descriptive and interpretive; does not seek to refute hypotheses but to illuminate meaning.
source_name: Yale University HRAF — Introduction to Fieldwork and Ethnography by Francine Barone

description: A structured method of data collection using standardized questionnaires administered to a sample of a population, designed following principles such as Dillman's Total Design Method (1978) to maximize response rates and data quality.
when_to_use: When you need to describe characteristics, attitudes, or behaviors of a large population quickly and cost-effectively; useful for estimating prevalence and correlations.
seeks_refutation: Partially — can be used to test hypotheses, but emphasis is on measurement and description; refutation depends on study design.
source_name: Don A. Dillman (1978) The Total Design Method for mail and telephone surveys

description: A randomized controlled experiment comparing two or more variants (A and B) to determine which produces a better outcome, widely used in digital products, marketing, and clinical trials.
when_to_use: When you can randomly assign users or subjects to treatment groups and need a causal estimate of an intervention's effect on a specific metric.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) with p-values and confidence intervals seeks to reject or fail to reject the null hypothesis.
source_name: Alex Deng — Statistical Analysis of A/B Tests (Causal Inference and Its Applications in Online Industry)

description: A research design that estimates causal impact of an intervention without random assignment, using techniques like regression discontinuity, interrupted time series, and nonequivalent control groups (Shadish, Cook & Campbell, 2002).
when_to_use: When random assignment is infeasible or unethical but you still need to estimate a causal effect; common in education, public health, and social policy.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicitly addresses threats to causal inference (internal validity) and seeks to rule out alternative explanations.
source_name: Shadish, Cook & Campbell (2002) Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Generalized Causal Inference

description: A structured approach that evaluates two or more methods, theories, or cases against shared criteria to identify similarities, differences, and contextual fit (Sartori, 1994).
when_to_use: When you need to justify a choice among alternatives, explain trade-offs, or demonstrate why one approach performs differently under specific conditions.
seeks_refutation: Yes — comparison against shared criteria can reveal weaknesses and disconfirm claims about superiority.
source_name: Comparative Research Methods (Esser, in Wiley Encyclopedia of Research Methods)

description: A statistical technique combining results from multiple independent studies to produce a pooled effect size estimate, increasing statistical power and resolving conflicting findings. Coined by Gene Glass in 1976.
when_to_use: When multiple studies on the same question yield varying results and you need a statistically rigorous pooled estimate of the overall effect.
seeks_refutation: Yes — explicit hypothesis testing (e.g., homogeneity of effect sizes) and identification of outliers seeks to detect and resolve contradictions.
source_name: Gene V. Glass (1976) Primary, Secondary, and Meta-Analysis of Research — American Educational Research Association presidential address

description: A structured communication technique using iterative rounds of anonymous expert surveys with controlled feedback to achieve consensus on a complex problem, originally developed at the RAND Corporation in the 1950s.
when_to_use: When no empirical evidence can settle a question, expert judgment is needed, and direct face-to-face meetings may be biased by group dynamics.
seeks_refutation: Partially — anonymous iteration allows experts to revise views in light of dissent, but it seeks convergence rather than refutation.
source_name: Linstone & Turoff (2002) The Delphi Method: Techniques and Applications; RAND Corporation origins

description: The use of multiple methods, data sources, theories, or investigators to study the same phenomenon in order to enhance validity, mitigate bias, and corroborate findings (Denzin, 1978).
when_to_use: When findings from a single method or source may be questioned and you need to cross-validate by combining qualitative and quantitative evidence or multiple independent datasets.
seeks_refutation: Yes — convergence across independent methods strengthens confidence; divergence actively exposes weaknesses and potential error in any single method.
source_name: Norman K. Denzin (1978) The Research Act — cited in JSTOR and Walden University review
